## 1. Executive Snapshot

- **CRITICAL — Two Google Gemini API endpoints go dark today (June 25).** `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` and `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` are deprecated effective today. Any production system still calling these endpoints will return errors starting now. Audit your codebase for these model IDs immediately — migration to `gemini-3.5-flash` or `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` is the correct path and takes under an hour.

- **Google officially delays Gemini 3.5 Pro to July** (confirmed today, June 25). Google cited quality refinements after early enterprise testing and agentic task feedback. The delay lands as four senior AI researchers — including Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer — have departed for OpenAI and Anthropic, compounding the narrative pressure. The previous ETB recommendation to "evaluate at GA this week" must be updated: plan for a July evaluation window.

- **OpenAI unveiled "Jalapeño" — its first custom LLM inference chip** (June 24), built with Broadcom in a record 9-month ASIC development cycle. The chip is designed specifically for LLM inference, is already running GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark in labs, claims superior performance-per-watt vs. state-of-the-art, and will deploy by end of 2026. This is OpenAI's most significant infrastructure move: breaking dependence on NVIDIA GPUs while simultaneously preparing IPO investors a full-stack compute story.

- **Fable 5 remains offline — Day 13 — but the legal landscape is shifting.** Legion LegalTech filed a federal lawsuit on June 23 arguing the export control directive is unlawful. Trump reportedly softened his position in an Axios interview (June 20) but the Commerce Department directive remains in legal force. Prediction markets place 57% probability of restoration before July 1. Simultaneously, new reporting (June 24) reveals that Anthropic's Mythos model found real vulnerabilities in classified US government systems during Project Glasswing testing — providing the most concrete public explanation of why the government acted. Maintain Opus 4.8 as the production standard.

- **A new wave of AI infrastructure companies emerged this week with landmark funding.** Mirendil ($200M seed, $1B valuation) — founded by ex-Anthropic/OpenAI/DeepMind/xAI researchers — is building systems that automate AI R&D itself: "the most important application of AI is AI." Engram ($98M) is building a learned memory layer that lets enterprise AI match frontier models using 1–10% of tokens. Runlayer ($30M, Vinod Khosla: "I want every available dollar") is becoming the governance layer for every corporate employee interacting with AI agents. These three companies represent the next infrastructure layer above frontier models — and the investment conviction signals the market believes this layer is the next $10B+ category.

## 2. Technologies Worth Watching

### Technology 1: OpenAI Jalapeño — First Custom LLM Inference Chip

**Category:** AI Infrastructure / Custom Silicon / Vertical Integration

**What happened?**
On June 24, 2026, OpenAI and Broadcom unveiled Jalapeño — OpenAI's first Intelligence Processor, a custom AI accelerator architected specifically around LLM inference requirements. The chip was co-developed from initial design to manufacturing tape-out in just nine months — a claim OpenAI describes as "the fastest ASIC development cycle ever achieved in high-performance advanced semiconductors." Engineering samples are running in OpenAI's labs, validated on GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark at production target frequency and power. Early testing shows "substantially better performance per watt" than current state-of-the-art (NVIDIA GPUs). Manufacturing: TSMC. System integration: Celestica. Full deployment: by end of 2026. The architecture is designed for "gigawatt-scale" data center deployments with Microsoft and other partners.

**Why it matters?**
Every major hyperscaler (Google TPU, Amazon Trainium, Meta MTIA) has built custom silicon to reduce NVIDIA dependency and lower inference costs. OpenAI entering this race — with a chip purpose-built for the specific inference patterns of its own models — closes the last remaining gap in OpenAI's full-stack story. The strategic implication is three-fold: (1) Inference costs for ChatGPT and Codex will drop, making aggressive pricing to enterprise customers feasible ahead of the IPO; (2) A chip optimized for OpenAI's own model architectures will deliver performance advantages that commodity NVIDIA hardware cannot; (3) The 9-month development timeline — using AI models to accelerate chip design — is itself a proof point that the "AI-assisted engineering" thesis is operational, not theoretical. For competitors: Anthropic is reportedly "weighing building an AI chip of its own," and Google's TPUs already exist. This is now a three-lab chip race.

**Business Value:**
(1) Jalapeño deployment will translate to OpenAI reducing per-token inference costs significantly by 2027 — expect API pricing pressure on competitors. (2) OpenAI's chip-to-model optimization creates a performance moat that pure model improvements cannot replicate. (3) For companies building on OpenAI APIs: lower inference costs in 2027 make GPT-class model calls economically viable for workloads currently too expensive (high-volume, real-time, low-latency tasks).

**Company Relevance:** 78 / 100

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Observe. No immediate action required. Monitor OpenAI API pricing announcements in H2 2026 for cost reduction signals linked to Jalapeño deployment. Factor into 2027 AI infrastructure cost modeling.

**Expected Impact:** Medium-term (pricing impact: H1 2027; competitive landscape: 12 months)

### Technology 2: Gemini API Deprecation + 3.5 Pro Delay — Immediate and Near-Term Risks

**Category:** Foundation Models / API Lifecycle / Infrastructure Risk

**What happened?**
Two developments from Google require immediate attention. First: `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` and `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` are deprecated and go dark **today, June 25, 2026**. Any system still calling these endpoints will fail. Migration path: `gemini-3.5-flash` or `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. Second: Google officially confirmed today (June 25) that Gemini 3.5 Pro's general availability has slipped from June to July 2026, citing quality refinements after early Antigravity platform and LMArena testing, with particular attention to token efficiency in long-horizon agentic tasks. The delay coincides with the departure of four senior AI researchers, including Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer, to OpenAI and Anthropic.

**Why it matters?**
The deprecation is an operational emergency — not a planning item. Systems using the deprecated model IDs are failing right now. The Pro delay matters strategically: the 2M-token full-codebase analysis use case is still coming, but not this week. For roadmap planning: update all Gemini 3.5 Pro evaluation windows from "June" to "July." Google has a pattern of slipping model releases (Gemini Ultra 1.5 slipped three months), so July remains the base case but a further slip cannot be ruled out.

**Business Value:**
(1) Immediate: audit codebase for deprecated model ID calls — every production integration calling `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` or `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` is broken today. (2) Planning: reschedule any Gemini 3.5 Pro evaluation work from this week to July. (3) The talent pressure context is relevant for long-term provider strategy — Google's research depth at the frontier is thinning.

**Company Relevance:** 92 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Act immediately — search codebase for deprecated model IDs today. Reschedule Pro evaluation to July. Continue using Gemini 3.5 Flash for production workloads.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (deprecation: today; Pro: July)

### Technology 3: Fable 5 Legal Challenge + Project Glasswing — The Deeper Story

**Category:** AI Governance / National Security / Regulatory Risk

**What happened?**
Day 13 of the Claude Fable 5 / Mythos 5 global suspension, with several significant developments on June 23–24:

- **Legion LegalTech filed a federal lawsuit on June 23** against the US government, arguing the export control directive is unlawful. Key arguments: existing export control laws don't cover hosted AI models or their outputs; the IEEPA Berman Amendment explicitly withholds authority to regulate informational materials; the directive contradicts Trump's own June 2 executive order which said nothing should "authorise a mandatory governmental licensing or preclearance requirement" for new AI models; and the government failed to consider less-restrictive alternatives.

- **Trump softened his position (Axios interview, June 20)**, stating he "no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat." However, the Commerce Department directive signed by Secretary Lutnick remains legally in force. Restoration requires formal Commerce Department withdrawal or a new authorization framework — neither has been announced.

- **Project Glasswing revealed (June 24):** A US official told the AP that Anthropic's Mythos model found vulnerabilities in classified US government systems in hours during a testing exercise — the most concrete explanation yet for the government's concern. Anthropic partnered with US intelligence agencies in this exercise, which adds nuance: the government simultaneously tested Mythos for its capabilities and then restricted it.

- **Prediction markets:** 57% probability of restoration before July 1 (Polymarket); ~70% before July 17 (Kalshi).

**Why it matters?**
The Project Glasswing reveal changes the analytical frame. The government's Fable 5 action is not simply a trade dispute or political conflict with Anthropic — it is a response to demonstrated evidence that Mythos-class models can find vulnerabilities in classified infrastructure in hours. The legal challenge may succeed (the statutory argument is substantive) but the underlying national security concern is real. The pattern being established: the US government is building an AI security review infrastructure (June 2 executive order, voluntary pre-release review agreements with OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Microsoft/xAI), and using Fable 5 as the enforcement example. Meta remains the only holdout on the voluntary review framework.

**Business Value:**
(1) Maintain Opus 4.8 as production standard — do not wait on Fable 5. (2) The legal challenge provides a legal theory that could restore access, but litigation moves slowly; plan without Fable 5 through at least July. (3) The Project Glasswing context suggests that when Fable 5 does return, it may come with new usage restrictions or mandatory access logging for cybersecurity use cases — architect accordingly.

**Company Relevance:** 94 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Observe closely. Continue Opus 4.8 as primary. Monitor the Legion LegalTech case (legal precedent if successful) and Anthropic's status page for restoration. Prepare for a mid-July restoration scenario rather than this week.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (restoration: July; legal precedent: 6–12 months)

### Technology 4: Mirendil — Self-Accelerating AI R&D

**Category:** AI Research / Meta-AI / Frontier Lab

**What happened?**
On June 24–25, 2026, Mirendil emerged from stealth with a $200 million seed round at a $1 billion valuation — one of the largest AI seed rounds in history. The company was founded by Behnam Neyshabur (former Anthropic researcher), Harsh Mehta, Shayan Salehian, and Tara Rezaei, leading a 20-person team from Anthropic, xAI, Google DeepMind, and OpenAI. The investment was co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins, with NVIDIA participating. Mirendil's mission: build AI systems that automate AI R&D itself — "self-accelerating AI." Their thesis: "the most important application of AI is AI itself." When this loop works, it becomes self-compounding: AI that improves AI improves faster than labs that rely on human researchers alone.

**Why it matters?**
This is the most strategically significant new company announcement of the week. Mirendil represents the thesis that frontier AI model improvement is itself automatable — and that the lab that automates it first creates a runaway improvement cycle. The $200M seed at $1B valuation, with a16z, Kleiner Perkins, and NVIDIA backing a 20-person pre-product team, is a signal about where the venture community believes the next leverage point in AI lies: not building better models for humans, but building systems that build better models autonomously. For context: Karpathy's mandate at Anthropic is explicitly "Claude-assisted pretraining" — the same thesis from within a frontier lab. Mirendil is the independent version of that bet.

**Business Value:**
(1) No immediate product decision — Mirendil has no commercial product. (2) Strategic signal: the 12–24 month implication is that frontier model capability improvement rates will accelerate significantly if any of these "self-improving AI" efforts succeed (Mirendil, Karpathy at Anthropic, Shazeer at OpenAI architecture research). (3) NVIDIA's participation as an investor is notable: they are betting on the lab that automates AI research will need massive compute — keeping NVIDIA central even in a self-improving AI world.

**Company Relevance:** 65 / 100

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Observe. No action required. Track Mirendil's first model release as a signal for the self-improving AI thesis becoming operational. If successful, it will materially shorten the time between model capability generations.

**Expected Impact:** Long-term (12–24 months to first model; 24–36 months to observable capability impact)

### Technology 5: Engram — Learned Memory Layer for Enterprise AI

**Category:** AI Infrastructure / Memory / Enterprise Efficiency

**What happened?**
On June 23, 2026, Engram emerged from stealth with $98 million in funding from General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, and Sequoia Capital, with Andrej Karpathy and Assaf Rappaport (Wiz CEO) as advisors. Engram builds a "learned memory layer" for AI: instead of sending an organization's full context in every API call, Engram pre-studies an organization's world and compresses its knowledge into a reusable model memory unique to each client. The result: models that match or outperform frontier AI using 1–10% of the tokens — a 10–100x token efficiency gain. Early commercial partners include Microsoft (testing inside Microsoft 365), Notion, and Harvey. The company is 13 people, less than a year old.

**Why it matters?**
Token cost is currently one of the primary constraints on enterprise AI adoption at scale. Long-lived agentic workflows (always-on agents, continuous monitoring, cross-session knowledge) consume tokens fast — Notion's Simon Last noted that "always-on agents across Notion workspaces can burn through tokens fast, even for simple task triage." Engram's model changes the unit economics: instead of paying for the full context window every call, organizations pay for a compressed memory that compounds in quality as the AI learns more about them. The 10x–100x token reduction claim is the highest-leverage enterprise AI efficiency improvement demonstrated in 2026. Karpathy's advisory role is a technical credibility signal given his pretraining expertise.

**Business Value:**
(1) If we run long-lived agents or high-volume AI workflows, Engram's 10–100x token reduction represents a direct cost reduction we can calculate against our current API spend. (2) The "memory compounds with use" model means early enterprise adopters build a proprietary knowledge advantage that compounds over time — a moat that commodity AI queries cannot create. (3) Microsoft 365 integration is the highest-relevance enterprise deployment path: if we use Microsoft 365 for knowledge work, the Engram partnership means memory-optimized AI assistance will be available in our existing tools.

**Company Relevance:** 80 / 100

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Evaluate. Request early access or pilot. Calculate our current monthly token spend on high-frequency agent workflows. If that spend is above $5K/month, the ROI case for Engram is immediate. Evaluation time: 1 week.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (cost reduction: weeks after integration; compounding memory value: 3–6 months)

### Technology 6: Agent Governance Infrastructure Matures — Runlayer, Snyk Evo ADS, Qodo

**Category:** AI Security / Agent Governance / Enterprise Infrastructure

**What happened?**
Three independent launches in the same week collectively define a new product category — Agent Governance Infrastructure:

- **Runlayer ($30M, Vinod Khosla)** (June 24): MCP gateway + identity controls + observability + agent workflows for enterprises. Gives every employee a governed path to use AI agents (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex) with approved MCPs, policy enforcement, and full audit trails. Khosla wanted "every available dollar" of the round.

- **Snyk Evo ADS** (June 23, GA June 29): Agentic Development Security — the enforcement layer inside agent workflows. Three layers: govern what agents use (MCP/tool approval), govern what agents do (real-time policy enforcement on agent behavior, blocking destructive actions before execution), and secure what agents generate (AI-generated vulnerability scanning at creation time).

- **Qodo governance capabilities** (June 23): Cross-Repo Code Review (impact analysis across repositories on every PR), Custom Rules Miner (auto-discovers coding standards from existing codebase behavior), and Skill Review Standards (centralized governance for agent skills). Trusted by Walmart, NVIDIA, Red Hat, Monday.com.

**Why it matters?**
The Sonar 2026 State of Code Developer Survey reported that 42% of committed code is now AI-generated or AI-assisted. AppSec Santa research found that 25% of AI-generated code samples contained critical security vulnerabilities. At 42% AI code contribution, code security is now an AI governance problem, not just a developer behavior problem. Runlayer, Snyk Evo ADS, and Qodo all converged on the same week because enterprise AI agent adoption has crossed the threshold where security and governance can no longer be addressed by policy alone — they require infrastructure.

**Business Value:**
(1) If we are deploying AI coding agents across our engineering team, Snyk Evo ADS (GA June 29) is the most targeted control layer — it operates inside the agent workflow, not downstream. (2) Runlayer is the right tool if our AI governance challenge is organization-wide (multiple teams, multiple AI tools, shadow AI risk) — it provides the centralized MCP gateway and audit trail. (3) Qodo's Cross-Repo Code Review addresses the specific risk of AI-generated changes breaking downstream dependencies that single-repo reviews miss.

**Company Relevance:** 85 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Evaluate Snyk Evo ADS this week (GA June 29). If we have engineering teams using Claude Code, Cursor, or GitHub Copilot at scale, Evo ADS is the most actionable immediate control. Evaluate Runlayer if organization-wide agent governance is an active concern. Evaluation time: 1 week each.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (Snyk: available June 29; Runlayer: deploy in days)

### Technology 7: AWS Lambda MicroVMs — Isolated Execution Primitive for AI-Generated Code

**Category:** Cloud Infrastructure / Serverless / AI Sandboxing

**What happened?**
On June 22, 2026, AWS announced the general availability of Lambda MicroVMs — a new serverless compute primitive built on Firecracker virtualization (the same technology powering over 15 trillion monthly Lambda invocations). Lambda MicroVMs provide VM-level isolation for executing user or AI-generated code, with near-instant launch and resume speeds and stateful execution for up to 8 hours. Key capability: each user or AI agent session gets its own dedicated MicroVM — no shared kernel, no shared resources, no risk of one agent's actions affecting another's. MicroVMs can be suspended with full memory and disk state preserved, then resumed in near-instant time. Available in US East (Virginia/Ohio), US West (Oregon), Europe (Ireland), Asia Pacific (Tokyo). Pricing: baseline compute while running; additional resources only when consumed.

**Why it matters?**
The 25% AI-generated code vulnerability rate reported by AppSec Santa means AI-generated code cannot be treated as trusted code — it must execute in isolation before being integrated into systems. Lambda MicroVMs create the execution primitive that makes "sandboxed AI agent execution" a cloud-native, serverless capability rather than a complex custom infrastructure problem. For any company building multi-tenant AI products (AI coding environments, agentic automation platforms, AI-powered data analytics), this eliminates the hardest infrastructure challenge: providing per-user/per-agent isolation at scale without managing VMs. The 8-hour stateful window is specifically designed for agentic AI sessions — coding agents that run for hours maintaining state.

**Business Value:**
(1) If we build any product where users or AI agents execute code, Lambda MicroVMs replace the custom sandboxing infrastructure we would otherwise need to build. (2) For our own internal AI agent workflows, MicroVMs enable per-agent isolation that prevents a misbehaving agent from affecting production systems. (3) The Firecracker-backed instant resume means agent sessions can persist across idle periods without burning compute costs — solving the "always-on agent" economics problem.

**Company Relevance:** 77 / 100

**Priority:** Medium

**Recommendation:** Pilot — identify one internal AI agent workflow or one product feature that currently runs AI-generated code without isolation. Migrate it to Lambda MicroVMs and measure: isolation confidence, launch latency, and cost vs. current approach. Evaluation time: 1–2 days.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (available now; product impact within 1–2 weeks of adoption)

---

### Technology 8: Cursor's 1.5 Trillion Parameter Model + Origin Git Platform

**Category:** Developer Tools / AI Coding / Vertical Integration

**What happened?**
At its Compile keynote on June 16, Cursor co-founder Michael Truell disclosed that Cursor is training its first fully self-built AI model — a 1.5-trillion-parameter system being pre-trained from scratch (not fine-tuned from an existing base) on xAI's Colossus supercomputer cluster using more than 100,000 GPUs. Unlike all previous Cursor models, which built on open-source foundations (most recently Kimi K2.5), this model begins from random initialization — giving Cursor full control of the entire training pipeline. Expected to ship within weeks of June 16. Simultaneously, Cursor announced Origin — an agent-first Git hosting and code collaboration platform with broad availability in fall 2026 (waitlist open now), built from scratch for parallel agent commits using NVMe+S3 storage architecture. Also announced: Cursor Mobile, an iOS public beta for managing AI agents remotely.

**Why it matters?**
Cursor is executing a vertical integration strategy of extraordinary speed and scope: it is simultaneously (1) closing the deal to become part of SpaceX/xAI, (2) training its first frontier model from scratch on SpaceX's compute, (3) building its own Git hosting platform to replace GitHub for agent-first workflows, and (4) extending to mobile. This is not a coding assistant company — it is becoming an end-to-end AI development platform. Origin is particularly significant: if agent-first developers migrate to Origin for Git hosting (because existing Git platforms weren't built for 100 parallel agent commits), Cursor controls the full stack from code generation to version control to deployment. The data implications for Grok are compounding: Cursor's coding data, now combined with a purpose-built Git platform, feeds the richest AI training dataset in the developer tools space.

**Business Value:**
(1) Cursor's own frontier model trained on Colossus may deliver coding-specific capabilities that generalist models cannot match — watch for benchmarks in coming weeks. (2) Origin represents a competitive threat to GitHub if it delivers meaningfully better agent workflow support — evaluate the waitlist. (3) The vertical integration trajectory (model + IDE + Git hosting) means the data portability risk of Cursor's SpaceX acquisition is compounding, not static — the longer engineering teams stay on Cursor, the more entrenched the data relationship becomes.

**Company Relevance:** 85 / 100

**Priority:** High

**Recommendation:** Evaluate — the Cursor vendor review should now include not just the SpaceX/data question but also the Origin platform decision. If Origin delivers meaningfully better multi-agent Git workflows, it will pull engineering teams into a deeper Cursor dependency before the acquisition close. Make the Cursor decision before Q3 2026.

**Expected Impact:** Short-term (model: weeks; Origin: fall 2026)

## 3. New Tools & Platforms

---

### GitKraken Kepler — Agentic Development Environment

**Category:** Developer Tools / Agent Orchestration / Engineering Platform

**What does it do?**
Kepler is GitKraken's Agentic Development Environment (ADE) — a standalone delivery engine for developers who are directing multiple AI coding agents rather than writing code themselves. Key capabilities: Tasks as a cross-repo coordination unit (database migration + API change + frontend update expressed as one Task across repos), Commit Composer (structures raw agent output into clean reviewable commits), Conflict Resolver and Auto-Rebasing (handles parallel agent conflicts as first-class operations), Multi-Agent Oversight (every agent in flight across every repo from one view), and Human-in-the-Loop Controls (routes decisions requiring judgment back to the developer with full context). Available on Windows, Mac, Linux, and browser.

**Who should use it?** Engineering teams already running multiple parallel AI coding agents who spend significant time shepherding branches, resolving conflicts, and managing agent output before it reaches PR.

**Business Use Case:** Reduce the coordination overhead tax of multi-agent coding — from managing 3–5 agents manually in multiple terminal windows to a single surface where all agent work is visible, conflict-resolved, and merge-ready.

**Estimated ROI:** GitKraken estimates 10+ hours per engineer per week in coordination overhead eliminated for teams running 5+ parallel agents. ROI is directly proportional to agent density — low value for 1-agent teams, high value for 10+ agent environments.

**Recommended Experiment:** Have 2–3 engineers currently running parallel Claude Code or Devin agents switch to Kepler for one sprint. Measure: time from agent-start to merged PR, and number of manual conflict resolutions required. Compare with previous sprint baseline.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 3–5 days

---

### Snyk Evo ADS — Agentic Development Security

**Category:** AI Security / Code Governance / DevSecOps

**What does it do?**
Snyk Evo Agentic Development Security (GA June 29) operates as a real-time security enforcement layer inside AI agent workflows — not as a post-hoc scanner. Three control layers: (1) Govern agent use — what tools, MCPs, and integrations agents are permitted to connect to; (2) Govern agent behavior — real-time policy enforcement on what agents do, blocking destructive actions before they execute; (3) Ensure trusted output — AI-generated vulnerability scanning at the moment of code creation, not in post-production review. Integrates natively with Claude Code-style agentic tools.

**Who should use it?** Engineering teams deploying AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, OpenHands) at any scale where AI-generated code is merged to production.

**Business Use Case:** Close the security gap created by AI-generated code (25% of AI-generated code samples contain critical vulnerabilities per AppSec Santa). Prevent agents from taking destructive infrastructure actions (deleting data, modifying access controls) that automated reviewers miss. Provide security team visibility into what agents are doing inside engineering workflows.

**Estimated ROI:** Prevents one significant AI-generated security incident per quarter for teams generating 10K+ AI lines/week. Given that AI security incidents typically cost 5–10x the cost of prevention, ROI is straightforward for any team with meaningful AI code volume.

**Recommended Experiment:** Install Evo ADS on one engineering team's workflow on June 29 (GA day). Run for 2 weeks without changing agent behavior. Review the telemetry: what tools are agents connecting to, what actions were flagged, what vulnerabilities were caught at creation. Use the findings to set permanent governance policies.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 2 weeks (1-day install + 2-week observation window)

---

### Engram — Learned Memory Layer for Enterprise AI

**Category:** AI Infrastructure / Memory / Token Efficiency

**What does it do?**
Engram pre-studies an organization's world (documents, workflows, code, communication patterns) and compresses its knowledge into a reusable, continuously improving model memory unique to each client. When AI agents or assistants query this memory instead of receiving full context, they match or outperform frontier models using 1–10% of the tokens. The memory compounds: the more the organization uses Engram, the richer the memory becomes, creating proprietary knowledge that general AI queries cannot replicate. Partners: Microsoft (365), Notion, Harvey.

**Who should use it?** Engineering teams running long-lived AI agents, legal teams with AI document review workflows, operations teams with high-frequency AI queries against large organizational knowledge bases.

**Business Use Case:** Cut AI API costs 10–100x for any workflow that currently re-sends the same organizational context on every call. Make always-on AI agents economically viable (instead of prohibitively expensive).

**Estimated ROI:** Direct calculation: take current monthly token spend on repeated-context workloads × 0.01 to 0.10. If current spend is $10K/month, Engram yields $9K–$9.9K in monthly savings. Plus the compounding quality improvement as memory improves.

**Recommended Experiment:** Identify the 3 highest-token-spend workflows where the same organizational context is re-sent repeatedly (e.g., code review agents that re-read documentation, support agents that re-load company knowledge). Request Engram early access and run a parallel token-spend comparison for 2 weeks.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 2 weeks

---

### Mistral OCR 4 — Structure-Aware Document AI, On-Premise Ready

**Category:** Document Intelligence / Data Sovereignty / Enterprise AI

**What does it do?**
Released June 23, 2026, Mistral OCR 4 is a structure-aware document intelligence model covering 170 languages. It returns paragraph-level bounding boxes alongside extracted text, block labels (titles, tables, signatures), and per-word and per-page confidence scores — not just raw text extraction. Critically: deploys as a single Docker container on any organization's own infrastructure with zero external API dependency. Available via Mistral API, Amazon SageMaker, and Microsoft Foundry. Integrated into Mistral's Search Toolkit for direct document-to-RAG pipelines. Available now; production webinar July 7.

**Who should use it?** Legal, compliance, finance, healthcare, and government teams that cannot route sensitive documents through third-party cloud APIs. Any team building document-to-AI pipelines that need structured output rather than raw text.

**Business Use Case:** Ingest contracts, regulatory filings, financial statements, or classified documents for AI analysis without the data ever leaving internal infrastructure. The structured output (bounding boxes, block labels) eliminates the preprocessing layer needed before feeding documents to RAG systems.

**Estimated ROI:** Eliminates third-party API dependency for document processing (data sovereignty ROI). Structured output eliminates custom preprocessing code (development ROI). 55% of enterprise inference now runs on-premises — Mistral OCR 4 positions for this majority architecture.

**Recommended Experiment:** Deploy Mistral OCR 4 as a Docker container (2 hours). Run 50 internal documents through it and compare output quality vs. current OCR tool. Evaluate: structure accuracy, language coverage, on-premise latency vs. cloud API latency.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1 week

---

### Runlayer — Enterprise AI Agent Governance Platform

**Category:** AI Governance / Security / Enterprise Infrastructure

**What does it do?**
Runlayer is an MCP gateway + identity controls + observability + agent workflow platform that gives every corporate employee a governed path to use AI agents. It provides: a catalog of 18,000+ MCP integrations with IT-approved subsets, custom MCP packaging of internal APIs and workflows, per-user identity and OAuth controls, real-time security scanning of tool calls and agent outputs, AI spend monitoring tied to teams and workflows, and shadow AI detection. Works across Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, and internal agents. Founded by the team that built Zapier MCP.

**Who should use it?** Companies where multiple teams are independently adopting AI agents (Claude Code, Cursor, ChatGPT Enterprise) faster than IT and security can govern them. Organizations that need MCP governance, centralized audit trails, and shadow AI visibility.

**Business Use Case:** Replace the fragmented reality of engineering/ops/security teams each configuring their own AI agent access (different MCP servers, different permissions, no central visibility) with a single governed platform that IT controls and employees still love using.

**Estimated ROI:** Risk reduction: eliminates shadow AI data exposure. Cost visibility: AI spend tied to teams and projects. Governance: pre-defined policies prevent agents from accessing unauthorized systems. Productivity: engineers spend less time on MCP configuration, more time on work.

**Recommended Experiment:** Audit current AI agent usage across the company for 1 week using Runlayer's shadow AI detection. Review: how many MCP servers are configured by individual engineers without IT knowledge, what data those MCPs access, and which AI clients are in use across teams. Use findings to build the governance policy.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1 week audit + 2 weeks rollout

### AWS Lambda MicroVMs — Serverless Isolated Execution for AI Agents

**Category:** Cloud Infrastructure / Serverless / AI Sandboxing

*(Full product description in Section 2, Technology 7.)*

**Who should use it?** Engineering teams building multi-tenant AI products, companies with internal AI agents executing code, and any team running AI agents that interact with production systems.

**Business Use Case:** Provide per-agent VM-level isolation without managing virtualization infrastructure — the missing compute primitive for safe multi-agent execution at scale.

**Estimated ROI:** Eliminates custom sandboxing infrastructure development (weeks of engineering). Reduces blast radius of agent errors to zero outside the MicroVM.

**Recommended Experiment:** Migrate one internal AI agent workflow to Lambda MicroVMs. Measure isolation confidence and cost vs. current approach.

**Estimated Evaluation Time:** 1–2 days

---

## 4. Global Technology Direction

### Where is the market moving?

**The defining pattern of June 25, 2026: AI is vertically integrating at every layer of the stack simultaneously — chips, models, memory, execution, governance — and the companies that own the most layers are pulling away from those that own fewer.**

OpenAI unveiled its first custom chip. Cursor is training its first frontier model on SpaceX's supercomputer while building its own Git platform. Anthropic's Mythos found vulnerabilities in classified government systems — demonstrating capabilities that make it both the most valuable and the most regulated AI in existence. Mirendil raised $200 million to build AI that improves AI. Google delayed its flagship model while talent exits to competitors. AWS shipped the execution primitive that makes safe AI agent deployment serverless.

In a single week, the AI industry advanced on six separate dimensions simultaneously: hardware, model training, memory efficiency, execution infrastructure, governance, and regulation. This is not a market in early adoption — it is a market in rapid maturation.

**Major company movements:**

- **OpenAI:** Jalapeño chip unveiled (June 24) — first custom inference processor, TSMC fabricated, Broadcom partner, Celestica system integration, deploying end of 2026. Partner Network launched ($150M, 300K certified consultants). ChatGPT advertising expanding to Brazil and Mexico (weeks away). OpenAI's pre-IPO strategy is clear: build the full stack (chip-to-model-to-product-to-partner-network-to-advertising) and price the IPO against a vertically integrated AI company, not just an API provider.

- **Anthropic:** Fable 5 / Mythos 5 offline, Day 13. Legion LegalTech lawsuit filed against US government. Project Glasswing revelation: Mythos found vulnerabilities in classified systems in hours. Karpathy's pretraining team is operational. Trump reportedly views Anthropic differently — but the directive stands. Anthropic is simultaneously the most capable, most regulated, and most legally embattled AI company in the world.

- **Google:** Gemini 3.5 Pro delayed to July (confirmed today). Two model endpoints deprecated today. Four senior AI researchers departed, including Gemini co-lead. Gemini 3.5 Flash in full production (fastest frontier model at $1.50/$9 per million tokens). Google Cloud growing 63% YoY. Google's product execution is strong; its research talent concentration is weakening.

- **SpaceX/Cursor:** 1.5T parameter model training on Colossus (100K+ GPUs). Origin agent-first Git platform in waitlist preview. Cursor Mobile iOS beta. SpaceX is not buying Cursor to run it as-is — it is rapidly building a vertically integrated AI development platform (model + IDE + Git hosting + mobile) that will compete directly with GitHub, Anthropic, and OpenAI in the developer ecosystem.

- **Meta:** The last major AI lab holdout on US government voluntary security reviews. Muse Spark (closed model) released in April. Meta's anomalous position — billions in AI capex, closed model, government holdout — suggests a strategic posture that differs fundamentally from OpenAI and Anthropic. Watch for resolution or confrontation before Q3.

- **Frontier labs emerging:** Mirendil ($200M, self-improving AI), Thinking Machines Lab (Mira Murati, $2B commitment), Safe Superintelligence (Ilya Sutskever, ~$5B valuation). A second tier of well-funded frontier labs is forming — funded at valuations that would have been impossible without the GPT-4 proof point. They represent the next wave of model capability competition.

**Industry shifts:**

1. **The inference cost war is beginning.** OpenAI's Jalapeño, Google's TPUs, Amazon's Trainium, and Anthropic weighing custom chips all converge on the same thesis: whoever can inference frontier-quality models most cheaply wins enterprise pricing competition. The companies that build their own silicon will be able to undercut those that don't. Expect a new wave of API pricing cuts in H1 2027.

2. **AI governance is becoming product, not policy.** Runlayer, Snyk Evo ADS, Qodo, and Thoughtworks Agent/works all launched this week. The market has validated that "we have an AI policy document" is insufficient — enterprises need infrastructure that enforces governance in real time, at the point of agent execution. This is a new enterprise software category, and it is being funded and shipped in parallel by multiple serious players.

3. **The memory efficiency layer is the next cost frontier.** Engram's 1–10% token reduction claim — validated by Microsoft, Notion, and Harvey — addresses the most immediate constraint on scaling enterprise AI: the cost of re-sending organizational context on every call. A 10–100x token reduction across high-frequency enterprise AI workflows changes the economics of AI adoption for organizations currently priced out by API costs.

4. **AI vertical integration is eliminating neutral ground.** This week: OpenAI builds its own chip. Cursor trains its own model and builds its own Git platform. Both eliminate dependencies that previously made their products neutral to the infrastructure beneath them. The same consolidation is happening in AI memory (Engram), governance (Runlayer), and execution (AWS MicroVMs). The "neutral AI plumbing" layer is being claimed by well-funded incumbents and startups.

5. **Government AI oversight is becoming a global force.** The US (voluntary pre-release review framework, Fable 5 enforcement), EU (AI Act August 2 enforcement now certain), India (RBI bank AI liability framework, comments due July 24), and Europe collectively alarmed by the Fable 5 incident all moved in the same week. AI regulation is no longer a single jurisdiction story — it is a simultaneous global regulatory tightening.

### What This Means For Our Company

1. **Two Gemini endpoints go dark today — this is the most immediate action item in this report.** Search your codebase for `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` and `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` right now. Every production call to these endpoints is failing. Migration is 1–2 hours. Do not wait.

2. **The AI governance infrastructure category has arrived — evaluate before it's mandated.** Snyk Evo ADS goes GA on June 29. If we have AI coding agents in production, this is the most actionable control available in the next five days. Runlayer addresses the organization-wide governance challenge if multiple teams are adopting AI tools independently. This week is the right time to get ahead of a governance gap, not catch up to one.

3. **Engram's token efficiency claim deserves a proof-of-concept against our highest-spend AI workflows.** A 10–100x token reduction on repeated-context workflows is the largest near-term ROI opportunity in AI cost management. The Microsoft/Notion/Harvey partnerships validate the claim isn't theoretical. Request early access this week.

4. **Cursor's vertical integration trajectory changes the vendor risk profile materially.** The acquisition closes in Q3 2026. By then, Cursor may also control Origin (the Git platform our agents commit to) and a frontier model trained on our code. The decision window to evaluate OpenCode or Claude Code as alternatives is now — not after close. Act this month.

5. **Gemini 3.5 Pro evaluation window shifts to July.** Remove any time-sensitive decisions or sprint commitments dependent on Gemini 3.5 Pro shipping this week. Update your AI roadmap to reflect July as the base case and August as the contingency.

## 6. CTO Decision Board

### START THIS WEEK

1. **Audit codebase for deprecated Gemini model IDs — today.** Run a search for `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` and `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` across all production code, infrastructure configs, and CI pipelines. Both endpoints went dark today (June 25). Every call returning 404 or model-unavailable errors should be treated as a production incident. Migration: replace with `gemini-3.5-flash` (recommended) or `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`. This is a 1–2 hour fix. Estimated time: 1–2 hours engineering.

2. **Update Gemini 3.5 Pro evaluation from "this week" to "July."** Any sprint plans, product roadmap items, or architectural decisions that were contingent on Gemini 3.5 Pro shipping by June 30 must be updated. The July base case is confirmed by Google today. Do not hold work in progress waiting on this model. Inform any teams with Pro-dependent roadmap items.

3. **Deploy Snyk Evo ADS observation mode on June 29 (GA day).** Snyk Evo Agentic Development Security goes generally available on Sunday June 29. On Monday June 30, deploy it in observation mode (no enforcement) across engineering AI agent workflows. For 2 weeks, it will log what tools agents are connecting to, what actions are being flagged, and what vulnerabilities are being caught at creation. The telemetry from those 2 weeks will define our governance policy — and you cannot see what you don't instrument. Installation time: 1 day.

### EVALUATE THIS MONTH

1. **Calculate Engram ROI against our top-3 highest-token AI workflows and request early access.** Pull API spend data for the last 30 days. Identify the 3 workflows with the highest token consumption where the same organizational context is being re-sent on every call (documentation-aware code review, support agents reading company knowledge, research agents re-ingesting the same corpus). Multiply that token spend by 0.01 (conservative 100x reduction) and by 0.10 (conservative 10x reduction). That range is the monthly savings potential. If either number is above $1K/month, request Engram early access and run a 2-week parallel comparison. Timeline: ROI calculation this week; early access request immediately.

2. **Make the Cursor vendor decision before the Origin waitlist fills.** This is the third consecutive report recommending a Cursor vendor review. The Compile announcements (1.5T model, Origin Git platform, Cursor Mobile) have materially increased the vertical integration risk since ETB-2026-06-24. The decision options remain the same: (a) accept SpaceX ownership with clear data terms; (b) transition to OpenCode (MIT, air-gapped, 7.5M users); (c) standardize on Claude Code (Anthropic-native, no SpaceX exposure). Set a decision deadline of July 15 — before the Origin waitlist context changes the evaluation. Estimated time: 1 week vendor review + 1 week pilot of alternative if needed.

3. **Implement a Runlayer shadow AI audit across the organization.** Before deploying governance, you need to understand what you're governing. Runlayer's shadow AI detection will surface: every MCP server configured by engineers without IT knowledge, every AI client in active use across teams, and every agent workflow running outside approved systems. This audit typically surfaces 3–5x more AI usage than is visible to platform and security teams. The audit itself is non-disruptive — no enforcement, only observation. Use findings to scope the governance program. Timeline: deploy audit mode this week; findings in 1 week.

### IGNORE FOR NOW

1. **Mirendil product evaluation.** Mirendil raised $200M and is building genuinely important technology, but it has no commercial product. Watching them closely is the right call; evaluating them as a vendor is premature by at least 18 months.

2. **OpenAI Jalapeño pricing implications.** Jalapeño deploys by end of 2026 at the earliest. API pricing changes driven by Jalapeño efficiency gains will not materialize until H1 2027. Do not change AI budget assumptions based on unquantified future cost reductions from a chip that hasn't shipped.

3. **Cursor's 1.5T parameter model benchmarks (until released).** Cursor claims their self-built model will ship within weeks. Until it ships and independent benchmarks exist, any evaluation of Cursor's model is speculation. Revisit when the model is available and benchmarked.

## 7. Trend Detection

*Compared against ETB-2026-06-13 through ETB-2026-06-24*

---

**Trend 1: AI Governance Is Catching Up to AI Capability — Now Institutionalizing Into Product Infrastructure**

Seventh consecutive report showing AI governance activity intensifying. This week it crossed a new threshold: governance is no longer a policy conversation — it is a product category with real deployment. Runlayer ($30M, Khosla conviction), Snyk Evo ADS (GA June 29), Qodo (enterprise customers: Walmart, NVIDIA), and Thoughtworks Agent/works all shipped or funded in the same week. Simultaneously: US voluntary security review framework (June 2 executive order) now signed by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, and xAI — only Meta is outstanding. EU AI Act August 2 enforcement is certain (Parliament formally approved). India's RBI released draft liability framework for bank AI. The governance infrastructure product category is being validated by funding, enterprise adoption, and regulatory pressure simultaneously.

**Confidence:** 99%

**Suggested Action:** Do not treat AI governance as a future planning item. Snyk Evo ADS (June 29) is the most immediate deployment opportunity. Runlayer audit mode is the most immediate intelligence opportunity. Both can be deployed this week with minimal disruption. Governance without infrastructure is just policy — and policy doesn't scale to 42% AI code contribution.

---

**Trend 2: AI Infrastructure Vertical Integration — Every Major Player Is Building More of the Stack**

Fourth consecutive report with evidence of AI vertical integration expanding. The week's data points: OpenAI unveiled custom inference chip (Jalapeño). Cursor trained a 1.5T parameter model from scratch on SpaceX's supercomputer and announced Origin (Git hosting). Anthropic is reportedly weighing custom chip development. Google has TPUs. AWS shipped Lambda MicroVMs (execution primitive). The pattern: every major AI platform is eliminating dependencies on the layer below it. OpenAI eliminates NVIDIA GPU dependency. Cursor eliminates Anthropic/OpenAI model dependency. AWS eliminates custom VM orchestration dependency. The companies that own the most layers will have the lowest marginal cost and the strongest moat.

**Confidence:** 94%

**Suggested Action:** When evaluating AI vendors, add "how much of the stack do they own?" as a forward-looking dimension. Vendors with full-stack ambition (OpenAI chip-to-model-to-product, SpaceX compute-to-model-to-IDE-to-Git) have long-term cost and capability advantages, but also higher lock-in risk. Vendors dependent on other layers (any tool that relies entirely on OpenAI or Anthropic APIs) are more portable but potentially cost-disadvantaged as stack owners compete on price.

---

**Trend 3: Autonomous Engineering Agents Are Crossing the Adoption Threshold — Infrastructure Is Standardizing**

Third consecutive report. New evidence this week: 42% of committed code is now AI-generated or AI-assisted (Sonar 2026 survey). GitHub Agentic Workflows (public preview, June 11) enables AI agent automation natively inside GitHub Actions. GitKraken Kepler launched as an ADE for managing 10+ parallel agents. Niteshift reported top customers merging 100+ PRs per week via coding agents. xAI launched `/goal` in Grok Build for long-running autonomous task execution. The standardization signal: we have gone from "AI generates code" (2024) to "dedicated infrastructure manages AI-generated code pipelines" (2026). This is the standardization phase that preceded broad enterprise adoption of cloud infrastructure in 2012–2015.

**Confidence:** 91%

**Suggested Action:** Track your team's AI code contribution rate as a KPI. If it is below 30%, you are behind market adoption and leaving productivity on the table. If it is above 40%, you need governance infrastructure (Snyk Evo ADS, Runlayer) to match the adoption rate. Neither position is stable without active management.

---

**Trend 4: AI Memory and Token Efficiency — The New Frontier of Enterprise AI Economics**

New trend detected this report. Engram ($98M, 1–10% token usage), combined with AWS Lambda MicroVMs (stateful execution enabling persistent agent memory), GitHub's persistent agent memory in Agentic Workflows, and the previous report's coverage of n8n 2.0 persistent memory — converge on a consistent signal: the next major AI efficiency gain is not in better models but in better memory. Organizations that figure out how to give AI persistent, organizational-context memory will operate their AI at 10–100x the token efficiency of those that don't. This is the equivalent of the move from stateless to stateful web applications in 2005–2010: technically unglamorous, but economically transformative.

**Confidence:** 82%

**Suggested Action:** Audit current AI workflows for stateless/stateful patterns: are AI agents being re-initialized with the same context on every call? If yes, this is a direct cost reduction opportunity. Engram is the most mature commercial solution. Evaluate alongside the AWS Lambda MicroVMs stateful execution capability for different points in the workflow stack.

---

**Trend 5: Frontier AI Talent Is Concentrating Into Three Nodes — Anthropic, OpenAI, and a New Independent Lab Tier**

Third consecutive report tracking this trend. New data this week: Mirendil ($200M seed) adds 20 researchers from Anthropic, xAI, DeepMind, and OpenAI to the independent lab tier. Engram's advisors include Karpathy (now at Anthropic). Shazeer at OpenAI. Previous reports: Karpathy from Eureka Labs to Anthropic; all 11 xAI co-founders departed. Google continues to execute at scale but is losing the individual researchers responsible for its foundational architecture work. A second tier of well-funded independent labs (Mirendil, SSI, Thinking Machines Lab) now exists alongside Anthropic and OpenAI. For capability trajectory: talent concentration at Anthropic (Karpathy) and OpenAI (Shazeer) is the 12–18 month leading indicator for model capability leaps.

**Confidence:** 85%

**Suggested Action:** Incorporate the "talent leading indicator" into model evaluation cadence. When Anthropic or OpenAI announce major model releases in Q3/Q4 2026, prioritize evaluation — Karpathy's pretraining work and Shazeer's architecture research will have had 6+ months to influence those releases. Those may be qualitative leaps, not incremental improvements.

---

## 8. Strategic Roadmap

### THIS MONTH (June 2026)

- **Today (June 25):** Search codebase for deprecated Gemini model IDs. Migrate within 2 hours. Update Gemini 3.5 Pro evaluation windows in roadmap to July.
- **By June 28:** Request Engram early access. Calculate token spend on top-3 AI workflows. Assess 10–100x reduction potential.
- **June 29 (Snyk Evo ADS GA):** Deploy Evo ADS in observation mode on engineering AI agent workflows.
- **By June 30:** Continue Cursor vendor review — decision deadline July 15. Define evaluation criteria: data handling under SpaceX, Origin waitlist interest, OpenCode vs. Claude Code alternative feasibility.
- **By June 30:** Deploy Runlayer shadow AI audit across organization. Surface all active AI agent usage.
- **Ongoing:** Monitor Anthropic status page daily for Fable 5 restoration (estimated July, ~57% before July 1). Maintain Opus 4.8 as production standard.
- **Ongoing:** Monitor Meta's resolution with US government voluntary AI security review framework (expected announcement within weeks).

### NEXT 90 DAYS (July–September 2026)

- **July:** Gemini 3.5 Pro expected GA — run full-codebase analysis use case as first test. This was the highest-priority evaluation deferred from June.
- **July:** Fable 5 expected restoration — prepare migration plan from Opus 4.8 back to Fable 5 for appropriate workloads. Evaluate new usage restrictions if any.
- **July 7:** Mistral OCR 4 production webinar — attend if document intelligence is a use case. Evaluate for sensitive document workflows currently using cloud APIs.
- **By July 15:** Finalize Cursor vendor decision: accept SpaceX ownership, transition to OpenCode, or standardize on Claude Code. Document data handling terms.
- **August 2:** EU AI Act — GPAI transparency and chatbot disclosure obligations activate. Mandatory: disclosure mechanisms must be live for all EU-facing AI chat interfaces and AI-generated content. Fines up to €15M or 3% global revenue.
- **August:** Cursor/SpaceX deal approaching close. Monitor for model access policy changes (will SpaceX prioritize Grok over Claude/GPT?).
- **Q3:** OpenAI IPO targeted (September). Monitor S-1 for API pricing structure and infrastructure cost disclosures. Adjust 2027 AI spend assumptions.
- **Q3:** AWS Lambda MicroVMs — if piloted this month, scale to any AI agent workflow executing untrusted code or interacting with production systems.

### NEXT 12 MONTHS (H2 2026 – H1 2027)

- **Model capability inflection points are the primary strategic variable.** Karpathy at Anthropic (Claude-assisted pretraining), Shazeer at OpenAI (architecture research), and Mirendil (self-improving AI systems) are all 12–18 month leading indicators. By H1 2027, one or more frontier model releases may represent qualitative, not just quantitative, capability leaps. Plan evaluation cycles in Q1 and Q3 2027 with this assumption.
- **The inference cost war will play out in H2 2026 – H1 2027.** OpenAI's Jalapeño deploys end-of-2026. Google's TPUs are already in production. Anthropic is evaluating custom silicon. The outcome: API pricing competition will intensify, and the floor on what is economically viable will drop. Re-evaluate AI workflow economics in Q1 2027 with updated cost assumptions.
- **Agent governance will become a compliance requirement, not a best practice.** The US (executive order framework), EU (August 2026 enforcement active), and India (RBI draft framework) are all converging on AI accountability as a regulatory mandate. Companies that build governance infrastructure now (Snyk Evo ADS, Runlayer, Qodo) will treat upcoming mandates as a configuration change. Companies that don't will treat them as a crisis.
- **The memory efficiency layer will become standard enterprise AI architecture.** Engram's $98M raise, Microsoft partnership, and 10–100x token efficiency claim will attract competitive responses from existing AI providers. By H1 2027, expect persistent organizational AI memory to be a standard feature in Microsoft Copilot, Notion AI, and enterprise AI platforms — the question is whether to build on a specialist (Engram) or wait for platform integration.
- **Two IPOs will change the capital landscape.** Anthropic (October 2026 target) and OpenAI (Q4 2026) going public will unlock post-IPO M&A capability at unprecedented scale. Expect acquisition waves in developer tools, data infrastructure, and enterprise software starting Q1 2027. Monitor tools we depend on for acquisition risk — the companies buying them will be Anthropic and OpenAI post-IPO.

*If we only execute one thing today, it should be: **Search your entire codebase right now for `gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview` and `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` — these endpoints went dark today (June 25), and every production system still calling them is failing silently.***
